comment_text.sn22.83:2.1: Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī was most famous for his conversation with Sāriputta in MN 24, on the strength of which he was said to be the best Dhamma speaker (AN 1.196). His verse is at Thag 1.4:1.7. For his mother’s name, see note on MN 86:12.12.
comment_text.sn22.83:2.3: “Because of grasping” (upādāya) rather than the commentary’s “derived from”, which takes an object in genitive.
comment_text.sn22.83:4.3: Note the equality in terms of address: a newly ordained monk and their teacher refer to each other as “reverend” (āvuso; see DN 16:6.2.1).

root_text.sn22.83:1.2: Tatra kho āyasmā ānando bhikkhū āmantesi:
root_text.sn22.83:1.4: “Āvuso”ti kho te bhikkhū āyasmato ānandassa paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn22.83:2.1: “Puṇṇo nāma, āvuso, āyasmā mantāṇiputto amhākaṁ navakānaṁ sataṁ bahūpakāro hoti.
root_text.sn22.83:2.3: ‘upādāya, āvuso ānanda, asmīti hoti, no anupādāya.
root_text.sn22.83:2.4: Kiñca upādāya asmīti hoti, no anupādāya?
root_text.sn22.83:2.5: Rūpaṁ upādāya asmīti hoti, no anupādāya.
root_text.sn22.83:2.9: viññāṇaṁ upādāya asmīti hoti, no anupādāya.
root_text.sn22.83:3.1: Seyyathāpi, āvuso ānanda, itthī vā puriso vā daharo yuvā maṇḍanakajātiko ādāse vā parisuddhe pariyodāte acche vā udakapatte sakaṁ mukhanimittaṁ paccavekkhamāno upādāya passeyya, no anupādāya;
root_text.sn22.83:3.2: evameva kho, āvuso ānanda, rūpaṁ upādāya asmīti hoti, no anupādāya.
root_text.sn22.83:3.6: viññāṇaṁ upādāya asmīti hoti, no anupādāya.
root_text.sn22.83:4.12: Puṇṇo nāma, āvuso, āyasmā mantāṇiputto amhākaṁ navakānaṁ sataṁ bahūpakāro hoti.
root_text.sn22.83:4.14: Idañca pana me āyasmato puṇṇassa mantāṇiputtassa dhammadesanaṁ sutvā dhammo abhisamito”ti.

translation_text.sn22.83:2.1: “Reverends, the venerable named Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī was very helpful to me when I was just ordained.
translation_text.sn22.83:2.3: ‘Reverend Ānanda, the thought “I am” occurs because of grasping, not without grasping.
translation_text.sn22.83:2.5: The thought “I am” occurs because of grasping form,
translation_text.sn22.83:3.1: Suppose there was a woman or man who was young, youthful, and fond of adornments, and they check their own reflection in a clean bright mirror or a clear bowl of water. They’d look because of grasping, not without grasping.
translation_text.sn22.83:3.2: In the same way, the thought “I am” occurs because of grasping form,
translation_text.sn22.83:4.1: What do you think, Reverend Ānanda?
translation_text.sn22.83:4.11: They understand: “… there is nothing further for this place.”’
translation_text.sn22.83:4.12: Reverends, the venerable named Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī was very helpful to me when I was just ordained.
translation_text.sn22.83:4.14: And when I heard this teaching from Venerable Puṇṇa son of Mantāṇī, I comprehended the teaching.”

variant_text.sn22.83:2.1: mantāṇiputto → mantāniputto (bj, pts1ed, mr); mantānīputto (sya-all, km)
